items.headline: Your Business Should Fund Your Life
items.angle: Frame the 'perfect day' not as a lucky vacation, but as the designed outcome of a specific business model. Contrast the feeling of being fully present offline with the typical founder's reality of being constantly tethered to their work.
hooks: Nothing better than watching your business run itself while you're completely offline.
Nothing better than taking a full week off, without checking your phone, and coming back to more revenue.
Nothing better than building something online that lets you spend most of your time offline.
Nothing better than being in the field, knowing the systems you built are working for you back home.
Nothing better than replacing yourself as the bottleneck in your own company.

But for most founders and experts, that sounds like a complete fantasy. Their reality is being chained to a laptop, constantly putting out fires, and feeling like the business owns them.
The goal was never to build a business that just makes money. The goal was to build a business that buys back your time, which is your most valuable asset.
This is not about magic. It is about intentionally designing systems from day one using simple AI and automation to handle the repetitive work that burns you out.
Think about your client onboarding, your lead nurture, your content systems. These are engines you can build once, that then run for you forever.
When those engines are running, you get to return to the field. You get to spend your time doing what you love, with the people you love.
This is the whole idea behind my program, FieldWork. We call it 'Build Online, Live Offline'. It is a practical model for freedom.

If you're an expert who wants to build a business that serves your life, not the other way around, comment the word 'FIELDWORK' below. I will send you my free guide on how to build your own freedom engine.

items.headline: The Two Games of Business
items.angle: Frame the modern work world as two different 'games.' Expose the common hustle-culture game as a trap and introduce Christian's 'FieldWork' philosophy as the new, smarter game to play for winning back your time and freedom.
hooks: There are two games in business. Most people are playing the wrong one.
The goal of the game isn't to win. It's to build a business that lets you stop playing.
Your work is a game. Are you playing for more points, or for more time?
The old game is over. Welcome to the new one.
This is the game nobody tells you you're playing. And it's costing you your life.
talking_points: There are two games you can play in business, and most founders are unknowingly playing the wrong one.
The first game is the 'Infinite Game'. The goal is more. More clients, more revenue, more hours. You trade your time for growth, and the game never ends.
The second game is the 'Finite Game'. The goal here isn't just growth, it's freedom. You play to build something that runs without you.
You build the systems, the engines, the automation once, so you can return to the field. That’s the entire point.
In the first game, the prize is a bigger business. In the second game, the prize is your life back.
I used to play the infinite game, and it burned me out. Now, my business is built to run while I spend weeks at a time offline, out on the water.
This new game is about using smart automation and AI to build assets that buy back your time.
I put together a guide that walks you through building the first three automated engines to start playing this new game.
If you want to learn how to play the Finite Game, just comment the word 'FIELD' below, and I'll send it right over.

items.angle: Frame the story around a moment of extreme burnout where your successful business felt more like a prison. Position the choice not as vice vs. virtue, but of 'hustle culture' vs. intentional living, leading to the creation of the FieldWork philosophy.
hooks: I had a successful business, but I was completely broke when it came to time.
My business was growing, but I was personally failing. This is the story of how I fixed it.
It was a Tuesday afternoon, and I was on a boat in the middle of paradise, completely miserable.
I built a business to be free. Then one day I realized it had become my cage.
Read this if you feel trapped by the very thing you built to set you free.
talking_points: A few years ago, I found myself sitting on a boat in one of the most beautiful places on earth, but my mind was a million miles away, stuck on emails and client problems.
I had built a business that was successful by every metric I was told to care about. The numbers were good, the clients were happy.
But I had to be 'on' all the time. I was tethered to my phone and laptop, and even when I was physically 'offline', my brain was still clocked in.
I was missing the actual life I was supposedly building all this for. I was becoming a ghost in my own story.
That day, I realized I had a choice. I could keep scaling this prison of my own making until I burned out completely.
Or, I could tear it down to the studs and rebuild it on a new foundation: one where the business served my life, not the other way around.
So I went all-in on systems, automation, and smart leverage. I built engines that could run and grow without my constant, hands-on effort.
I built it so I could return to the field. So I could be present for the moments that actually matter.
That’s the whole idea behind FieldWork. It's not about working less, it's about working smarter so you can live more.
If you want to learn how to build a business that buys back your time, just comment 'FIELDWORK' below and I'll send you a free guide that breaks down the model.

Add a clear, text-based call to action on the screen for the final 5 seconds: 'Comment "FIELD" for the free guide'.
Ensure the entire video has clear, auto-generated captions for viewers watching with sound off.

items.headline: The Unscalable Secret
items.angle: Take the same 'single truth' format but apply it to business design instead of marketing. Frame the core FieldWork idea, that you should automate the scalable parts of your business to free you for the unscalable parts of your life.
hooks: Your best work should be unscalable.
The goal of your business is to let you disappear.
Stop trying to automate your genius.
I built a business that runs itself so I could get back to my life.
Fire yourself from any job a system could do better.
talking_points: Your best work, the thing that only you can do, should be completely unscalable.
There is an obsession in the online world with scaling everything. Automating every task, systemizing every conversation, trying to make it all run like a perfect machine.
The problem is, that's how you build a business that you are trapped inside. You become its most important, and most overworked, component.
The real goal is different. It is to build systems that handle the 80% of repetitive, scalable work, so you can pour all your energy into the 20% that truly matters.
For me, that unscalable work is being in the field with a camera, or connecting with a client one on one, or just having a new idea on a long hike.
That is where your unique genius lives. It is what creates real value, and it is what actually fills you up instead of draining you.
The whole point of building an online business is to free you up to do more of that. To build online, so you can truly live offline.
I put together a guide on the first three systems we help our clients build to make this a reality. If you want it for free, just comment 'SYSTEM' below and I will send it over.

items.headline: Before Your Business Owned You
items.angle: Connect the feeling of being trapped by your business to the original, freer vision you had when you started. Frame the current grind not as maturity, but as a detour from the real goal: a business that serves your life, not the other way around.
hooks: This was the goal before the hustle became the goal.
Remember the business you wanted before you built the one you have?
This was me before my business started running my life.
Remember when 'freedom' was the whole point of this?
This was the vision before I learned the 'right' way to build.
talking_points: This was the business I wanted to build before the hustle became the goal.
Before every new client meant less time, not more.
Before scaling just meant working weekends.
Before the inbox felt more important than the outdoors.
At some point, we start calling this 'paying your dues'.
But what really happens is the original vision gets buried.
The instinct to build an asset that serves you, not a job that owns you.
The desire to use your skills to buy back your time, not sell it all.
That part of the dream doesn't disappear. It just gets buried under meetings and manual tasks.
And when you feel that pull watching someone log off at 2 PM to go for a hike or take a month to travel, that's not jealousy. It's a reminder.
The original reason you started is still there, waiting for you. If you want the plan to get back to it, comment 'FieldWork' and I'll send it over.

Truth: The original vision is just buried, not gone.
Reframe: Seeing others live freely is not envy, it is a reminder.
Resolution: Your original reason for starting is still achievable.
CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' for the plan to get it back.
guidance: Show B-roll of you outdoors, enjoying life (kayaking, hiking, by a fire). This is the 'after'. Contrast it with quick cuts of someone (or you) looking stressed at a laptop.
Use clean, bold on-screen text for the key phrases like 'Before the inbox felt more important than the outdoors' and 'That's not envy. It's a reminder.'
The audio should be a thoughtful, slightly melancholic but ultimately hopeful instrumental track that builds momentum towards the end.
Film the final call to action as a direct to camera piece, making eye contact and speaking calmly and clearly.
The final frame should have the text 'Comment "FieldWork"' clearly visible on screen as you finish speaking.

items.headline: Scaling Your Freedom
items.angle: Use the 'X vs. Y' structure to contrast the traditional hustle culture goal of scaling a business with the FieldWork goal of scaling your personal freedom. Frame the business not as the end goal, but as the engine for an offline life.
hooks: Scaling your business is cool but scaling your free time hits different.
A full calendar is cool but an open map hits different.
Hitting inbox zero is cool but seeing zero bars of cell service hits different.
Building an online business is cool but building one that runs while you're offline hits different.
Your first million dollars is cool but your first month completely unplugged hits different.
talking_points: Scaling your business is cool, but scaling your free time? That hits different.
We're all taught to chase growth, more clients, more revenue, a bigger team.
But for so many founders and experts, that growth just builds a bigger, more demanding cage.
You end up with a successful business but a life you never have time to actually live.
What if the goal was different? What if we built our businesses specifically to buy back our time?
This is about using smart automation and AI to build systems that work for you, so you don't always have to.
Think of an automated client onboarding process, or an AI content engine that runs on its own. You build it once, and it serves you forever.
That is what gives you the freedom to shut the laptop and return to the field, to the things and people you love.
I put our entire framework for this into my program, FieldWork.
If you want the guide on how to build a business that runs itself so you can live offline, just comment the word 'FieldWork' below and I'll send it over.
